---
title: "Title of the first paper"
---

## Preface {.unnumbered}

::: {.contribution}
Briefly describe the candidate's contribution to this paper, e.g.: I designed the study, collected the data, performed the analysis, and wrote the manuscript. Co-author A advised on the methodology.
:::

State the publication status of the paper here (e.g., *published in Journal Name, 2024*; *under review at Journal Name*; *in preparation*).

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

\begin{quote}
\itshape
State the question, methods, principal findings, and contribution in 150--250 words. The abstract is rendered in italic and indented to match the conventional layout of a paper-format abstract.
\end{quote}

## Introduction

Set up the research question. The literature on this topic has grown rapidly [@smith2024example]. A textbook framing is provided by [@example2020book]; the present paper extends this by [your contribution]. A complementary perspective is offered by [@example2022chapter].

## Methodology

The model is estimated by ordinary least squares. The estimating equation is:

$$
y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_{1i} + \beta_2 x_{2i} + \varepsilon_i
$$ {#eq-paper1-ols}

where $\varepsilon_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ are independent and identically distributed error terms. Equation @eq-paper1-ols is the workhorse specification.

Inline mathematics also work --- for example, the t-statistic is $t = \hat{\beta} / \text{SE}(\hat{\beta})$.

## Data

The sample comprises $n = 240$ observations drawn from three sources. Descriptive statistics are reported in Table @tbl-paper1-desc.

| Variable        |   Mean |    SD |    Min |    Max |
|:----------------|-------:|------:|-------:|-------:|
| Age             |   27.3 |   3.1 |     22 |     39 |
| Programme year  |    2.4 |   1.0 |      1 |      4 |
| Outcome score   |    4.8 |   1.2 |    1.5 |    6.9 |

: Descriptive statistics of the sample. {#tbl-paper1-desc}

## Results

Figure @fig-paper1-dist shows the distribution of the outcome variable.

```{r}
#| label: fig-paper1-dist
#| fig-cap: "Distribution of the outcome variable."
#| fig-width: 5
#| fig-height: 3.2
#| echo: false
set.seed(42)
y <- rnorm(240, mean = 5, sd = 1.2)
hist(y, breaks = 20, col = "#5b1a8c", border = "white",
     main = "", xlab = "Outcome", ylab = "Frequency")
```

The estimated coefficients are reported in Table @tbl-paper1-est.

| Coefficient | Estimate | Std. error | t-value |  p-value |
|:------------|---------:|-----------:|--------:|---------:|
| Intercept   |     4.92 |       0.15 |    32.8 | < 0.001  |
| $x_1$       |     0.34 |       0.10 |     3.4 |   0.001  |
| $x_2$       |    -0.18 |       0.10 |    -1.8 |   0.073  |

: Estimated regression coefficients from the specification in Equation @eq-paper1-ols. {#tbl-paper1-est}

The slope on $x_1$ is positive and significant at the 1% level [@smith2024example].

## Discussion

Interpret the results. Discuss what the findings imply for theory and practice [@example2023conf].

## Conclusions

Summarise the contribution of this paper.

## References {.unnumbered}

The bibliography below is auto-generated from `references.bib` and the citations used above.

::: {#refs}
:::

## Appendices {.unnumbered}

### 2.A Sample survey instrument {.unnumbered}

Reproduce the survey instrument used to collect the primary data.

### 2.B Robustness checks {.unnumbered}

Document any robustness checks. Use sub-sub-sections for grouped material:

#### 2.B.1 Excluding outliers {.unnumbered}

Outlier exclusion does not change the headline result.

#### 2.B.2 Bootstrap standard errors {.unnumbered}

Bootstrap standard errors confirm the parametric inferences.
